column1: # External unit documentation loaded by the Ruby and compiled REPLs.

column2: metre — SI base unit of length, defined via the speed of light c
column3: From Greek metron, “measure”, through French mètre.
column4: The 1983 definition fixed the speed of light at exactly 299,792,458 metres per second.

column2: kilogram — SI base unit of mass, defined via the Planck constant h
column3: Greek khilioi, “thousand”, plus gramme.
column4: Since 2019 the kilogram is defined by fixing the Planck constant rather than by a metal artefact.

column2: gram — one thousandth of a kilogram
column3: From Late Latin gramma, a small weight, ultimately Greek grammē.
column4: The gram was an original metric mass unit; modern SI treats the kilogram as the base unit.

column2: second — SI base unit of time, defined by the caesium-133 hyperfine transition
column3: From Latin secunda pars minuta, the “second diminished part” of an hour.
column4: The atomic definition was adopted in 1967 and refined in wording without changing its duration.

column2: ampere — SI base unit of electric current
column3: Named for French physicist André-Marie Ampère.
column4: Since 2019 it is defined by fixing the elementary charge.

column2: kelvin — SI base unit of thermodynamic temperature
column3: Named for William Thomson, Lord Kelvin.
column4: Since 2019 it is defined by fixing the Boltzmann constant.

column2: mole — SI base unit of amount of substance
column3: From German Mol, shortened from Molekül.
column4: Since 2019 one mole contains exactly 6.02214076×10²³ specified entities.

column2: candela — SI base unit of luminous intensity
column3: Latin candela, “candle”.
column4: The modern photometric definition fixes the luminous efficacy of 540 THz radiation.

column2: hertz — one cycle per second
column3: Named for physicist Heinrich Hertz.
column4: The IEC adopted hertz in 1933; the CGPM adopted it for SI in 1960.

column2: newton — SI derived unit of force, kg·m/s²
column3: Named for Isaac Newton.
column4: The name newton was adopted internationally in 1948.

column2: pascal — SI derived unit of pressure, N/m²
column3: Named for Blaise Pascal.
column4: The pascal was adopted as the SI pressure name in 1971.

column2: joule — SI derived unit of energy, N·m
column3: Named for James Prescott Joule.
column4: The joule became an international electrical-mechanical energy unit in the late nineteenth century.

column2: watt — SI derived unit of power, J/s
column3: Named for James Watt.
column4: The watt was adopted by the British Association in 1882 and standardized internationally in 1889.

column2: volt — SI derived unit of electric potential
column3: Named for Alessandro Volta.
column4: The volt was adopted by the first International Electrical Congress in 1881.

column2: ohm — SI derived unit of electrical resistance
column3: Named for Georg Ohm.
column4: The ohm was adopted internationally in 1881 and later incorporated into SI.

column2: petabyte — 10¹⁵ bytes
column3: SI prefix peta, from Greek pente, “five”, marking the fifth power of one thousand.
column4: The SI prefix peta was adopted in 1975; decimal storage usage was standardized separately from IEC pebibytes.

column2: electronvolt — energy gained by one elementary charge through one volt
column3: Coined from electron plus volt.
column4: Widely used in atomic and particle physics; it became exact after the 2019 SI fixed the elementary charge.

column2: informal length equal to 1.7018 metres
column3: Named for Oliver R. Smoot, used bodily as the measuring rod.
column4: MIT students measured the Harvard Bridge in 1958 as 364.4 smoots plus or minus one ear.

column2: banana equivalent dose — approximately 0.1 microsievert
column3: Named for the familiar fruit used to communicate potassium-40 exposure.
column4: The informal comparison was popularized in radiation-safety communication in the 1990s.

column2: atomic unit of energy, approximately 27.211 electronvolts
column3: Named for Douglas Hartree.
column4: Introduced with atomic-unit methods for quantum calculations.

column2: atomic unit of length for the hydrogen ground state
column3: Named for Niels Bohr.
column4: Derived from the 1913 Bohr model and maintained as a CODATA atomic constant.

column2: rest mass of an electron
column3: Named for the electron, from Greek elektron, “amber”.
column4: A measured particle constant periodically refined by CODATA.

column2: rest mass of a proton
column3: Proton comes from Greek prōtos, “first”.
column4: A measured particle constant periodically refined by CODATA.

column2: rest mass of a neutron
column3: From Latin neuter, “neither”, referring to its lack of electric charge.
column4: A measured particle constant periodically refined by CODATA.

column2: degree Celsius — an absolute temperature point with the ice point near zero
column3: Named for Swedish astronomer Anders Celsius.
column4: Celsius proposed a centigrade scale in 1742; its modern relation to kelvin is exact.

column2: degree Fahrenheit — an absolute temperature point
column3: Named for instrument maker Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit.
column4: Fahrenheit published his mercury-thermometer scale in 1724; its modern relation to kelvin is exact.

column2: kelvin temperature difference
column3: Named for William Thomson, Lord Kelvin; delta marks a difference rather than a point.
column4: A kelvin interval has the same exact size as a Celsius interval.

column2: degree Celsius temperature difference
column3: Named for Anders Celsius; delta marks a difference rather than a point.
column4: Celsius intervals were separated from absolute Celsius readings here to prevent affine arithmetic mistakes.

column2: degree Fahrenheit temperature difference
column3: Named for Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit; delta marks a difference rather than a point.
column4: A Fahrenheit interval is exactly five ninths of a kelvin interval.

column2: lumen — SI unit of luminous flux
column3: Latin lumen, “light”.
column4: The lumen predates SI and was incorporated as the candela-steradian derived unit.

column2: lux — SI unit of illuminance, one lumen per square metre
column3: Latin lux, “light”.
column4: The name lux was recommended internationally in the late nineteenth century and incorporated into SI.

column2: unit of luminance equal to one candela per square metre
column3: From Latin nitere, “to shine”.
column4: The non-SI name became common in display engineering; candela per square metre remains the SI expression.

column2: gray — absorbed dose of one joule per kilogram
column3: Named for radiobiologist Louis Harold Gray.
column4: Adopted by the CGPM in 1975 as the special SI name for absorbed dose.

column2: sievert — equivalent or effective radiation dose of one joule per kilogram
column3: Named for medical physicist Rolf Maximilian Sievert.
column4: Adopted in 1979 to distinguish biological dose weighting from absorbed dose measured in grays.

column2: roentgen equivalent man — 0.01 sievert
column3: An acronym for roentgen equivalent man.
column4: Introduced for radiation protection before the sievert; retained as a non-SI unit.

column2: radian — plane angle subtending an arc equal to the radius
column3: A shortening of radius.
column4: The name was coined in the nineteenth century and the radian is the coherent SI unit of plane angle.

column2: steradian — SI unit of solid angle
column3: Greek stereos, “solid”, plus radian.
column4: The term was introduced in the nineteenth century and adopted into SI.

column2: parts per million — ratio of 10⁻⁶
column3: A literal abbreviation of parts per million.
column4: Widely used for trace concentrations; explicit ratios are preferred when ambiguity about mass or volume matters.

column2: kilograms per cubic metre — SI mass density
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: The coherent SI density unit follows directly from kilogram and metre.

column2: cubic metres per second — SI volumetric flow rate
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: Used in fluid mechanics and hydrology as the coherent volumetric flow unit.

column2: litres per minute — practical volumetric flow rate
column3: Litre derives from French litre, historically related to litron.
column4: Common in medicine, process engineering, and gas flow where cubic metres per second is inconveniently large.

column2: kilograms per second — SI mass flow rate
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: Used as the coherent mass-flow unit in thermodynamics and process engineering.

column2: joules per kelvin — heat capacity or entropy
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: Heat capacity and entropy share this unit but represent different physical quantities.

column2: joules per kilogram-kelvin — specific heat capacity
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: The coherent specific-heat unit replaced calorie-based conventions in SI work.

column2: watts per metre-kelvin — thermal conductivity
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: Used for Fourier-law thermal conductivity in engineering and materials science.

column2: watts per square metre — heat or irradiance flux
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: Used for power distributed over area in heat transfer and radiometry.

column2: volts per metre — electric-field strength
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: One of the standard coherent expressions for electric field in SI.

column2: amperes per square metre — electric-current density
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: Used for current distributed across a conductor cross-section.

column2: ohm metre — electrical resistivity
column3: Named from the ohm, honoring Georg Ohm, times metre.
column4: The coherent SI resistivity unit is the reciprocal counterpart of siemens per metre.

column2: siemens per metre — electrical conductivity
column3: Named for Werner von Siemens, divided by metre.
column4: The coherent SI conductivity unit is reciprocal ohm metre.

column2: coulombs per cubic metre — volume charge density
column3: Named from the coulomb, honoring Charles-Augustin de Coulomb.
column4: The coherent SI expression measures electric charge distributed through volume.

column2: newtons per metre — surface tension
column3: Named from the newton divided by metre.
column4: Used in the force-per-length form of surface tension.

column2: kilograms per metre — linear mass density
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: Used for cables, fibres, rails, and other distributed masses.

column2: kilograms per square metre — areal mass density
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: Used for paper grammage, coatings, textiles, and structural sheets.

column2: joules per cubic metre — energy density
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: Dimensionally equal to a pascal, but semantically used for stored energy per volume.

column2: joules per kilogram — specific energy
column3: A compositional SI expression.
column4: Dimensionally equal to absorbed dose, but semantically used for energy per mass rather than ionizing-radiation dose.

column2: moles per mole — amount fraction
column3: A compositional ratio of like amount units.
column4: The explicit mol/mol spelling avoids ambiguity in ppm-style concentration reporting.

column2: katals per cubic metre — catalytic activity concentration
column3: Katal comes from catalysis, ultimately Greek katalysis, “dissolution”.
column4: Used for catalytic activity distributed through a volume.

column2: candelas per square metre — SI luminance expression
column3: Candela is Latin for “candle”.
column4: This SI expression is commonly called a nit in display specifications.

column2: lux second — luminous exposure
column3: Lux is Latin for “light”, multiplied by second.
column4: Used in sensitometry and exposure measurement as illuminance integrated over time.

column2: lumen second — quantity of light
column3: Lumen is Latin for “light”, multiplied by second.
column4: Used for time-integrated luminous flux, including flash output.

column2: radians per second — angular velocity
column3: Radian derives from radius.
column4: The coherent SI angular-velocity expression makes the angular kind explicit.

column2: radians per second squared — angular acceleration
column3: Radian derives from radius.
column4: The coherent SI angular-acceleration expression makes the angular kind explicit.

column2: metres per second cubed — jerk
column3: Jerk is the established mechanics term for the time derivative of acceleration.
column4: Used in motion planning, ride comfort, and control systems.

column2: kilogram metres per second — momentum
column3: Latin momentum, “movement” or “moving power”.
column4: The coherent SI momentum expression follows from mass times velocity.

column2: newton second — impulse
column3: Latin impulsus, “a pushing against”.
column4: Impulse is force integrated over time and equals change in momentum.

column2: newton metre — torque
column3: Torque comes from Latin torquere, “to twist”.
column4: The explicit newton-metre spelling distinguishes rotational moment from joules of energy despite identical SI exponents.

column2: joules per kelvin tagged as heat capacity
column3: Heat comes from Old English hætu; capacity from Latin capacitas.
column4: The semantic spelling prevents heat capacity from being added to entropy solely because both use J/K.

column2: joules per kelvin tagged as thermodynamic entropy
column3: Coined by Rudolf Clausius from Greek en, “in”, and tropē, “transformation”.
column4: Clausius introduced entropy in 1865; the semantic spelling distinguishes the state quantity from heat capacity.

column2: joules per kilogram tagged as specific energy
column3: Specific denotes a quantity per unit mass.
column4: The semantic spelling prevents ordinary specific energy from being treated as radiation absorbed dose.

column2: becquerel — one radioactive decay per second
column3: Named for physicist Henri Becquerel.
column4: The CGPM adopted the becquerel in 1975; Tungsten retains decay as a compositional event kind rather than identifying it with cycles.

column2: bits per second per hertz — spectral efficiency
column3: Bit was coined from binary digit; hertz honors Heinrich Hertz.
column4: A communications metric describing information rate per unit bandwidth.

column2: joules per operation — computational energy efficiency
column3: Operation is shortened to op in computing.
column4: Used to compare the energy cost of processors, accelerators, and algorithms.

column2: joules per token — model-inference energy intensity
column3: Token derives from an item standing for another item; NLP adopted it for text pieces.
column4: A practical modern metric for language-model inference efficiency.

column2: bytes per floating-point operation — computational intensity reciprocal
column3: FLOP abbreviates floating-point operation.
column4: Used in roofline-style performance analysis to describe data traffic per arithmetic operation.

column2: kilograms of carbon-dioxide equivalent
column3: CO₂e means carbon dioxide equivalent, aggregating greenhouse gases by warming effect.
column4: Greenhouse-gas accounting uses CO₂e to report unlike gases on a shared basis.

column2: grams of carbon-dioxide equivalent
column3: CO₂e means carbon dioxide equivalent.
column4: A convenient smaller-scale greenhouse-gas accounting unit.

column2: grams CO₂e per kilowatt-hour — electricity carbon intensity
column3: A compositional greenhouse-gas and energy expression.
column4: Widely used to compare electricity grids and generation technologies.

column2: grams CO₂e per passenger-kilometre — transport carbon intensity
column3: Passenger-kilometre combines one passenger with one kilometre travelled.
column4: Used to compare per-passenger emissions among transport modes.

column2: CSS reference pixel — one ninety-sixth of a CSS inch
column3: Pixel is a blend of picture and element.
column4: CSS standardized the reference pixel and fixes 96 px per CSS inch for absolute-unit conversion.

column2: dots per inch — spatial resolution
column3: Dot plus the abbreviation for inch.
column4: Long used for print and display addressability; one dpi is one reciprocal inch.

column2: dots per CSS pixel — resolution unit equal to 96 dpi
column3: An abbreviation of dots per pixel.
column4: CSS Values and Units defines dppx for resolution media queries.

column2: CSS root-em — size relative to the root element font
column3: Rem abbreviates root em; em comes from the typographic em square.
column4: Introduced by CSS to make font-relative sizing independent of nested element font sizes.

column2: one percent of viewport width
column3: An abbreviation of viewport width.
column4: CSS introduced viewport-percentage lengths for responsive layouts.

column2: one percent of viewport height
column3: An abbreviation of viewport height.
column4: CSS introduced viewport-percentage lengths for responsive layouts.

column2: one person working for one hour
column3: A transparent compound of person and hour.
column4: Used in project planning while preserving the distinction between elapsed time and aggregate effort.

column2: quality-adjusted life year
column3: An acronym of quality-adjusted life year.
column4: Health economics developed QALYs to combine survival duration with a health-quality weight.

column2: relative software-estimation unit
column3: Named from user stories and an abstract point scale.
column4: Popularized by agile planning as a team-relative measure rather than a duration.

column2: milligrams of glucose per decilitre
column3: A compositional clinical concentration expression scoped explicitly to glucose.
column4: Common in United States blood-glucose reporting; conversion depends on glucose molar mass.

column2: millimoles of glucose per litre
column3: A compositional amount-concentration expression scoped explicitly to glucose.
column4: Common internationally for blood-glucose reporting and convertible to mg/dL only when the analyte is known.

column2: Mach number in dry air at 20 degrees Celsius using 343 m/s
column3: Named for physicist Ernst Mach.
column4: This contextual spelling freezes an approximate reference sound speed instead of pretending Mach has one universal linear conversion.
